region_of_origin: India (South Indian origin, served in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Mixed rice dish — spiced basmati rice with deep-fried cauliflower

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002694). No specific Gobi 65 Biryani entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers rice dishes broadly. No specific biryani or Gobi 65 Biryani code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for biryani or Gobi 65 Biryani.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Gobi 65 Biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice topped with crispy deep-fried cauliflower florets.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Biryani / Cauliflower Biryani — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) entries for cooked basmati rice, deep-fried cauliflower (Gobi 65 preparation), and biryani spice blend
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Gobi 65 Biryani. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked basmati rice (~65% by weight), deep-fried cauliflower Gobi 65 style (~30% by weight), and biryani masala/oil (~5% by weight). Energy and macros are weighted composites. Secondary cross-check performed against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked basmati rice (FDC ID 169704) and cauliflower (FDC ID 169986).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of basmati rice and mixed vegetable rice dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gobi 65 Biryani. GI estimated at 55 based on University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked basmati rice (GI ~50–58, mean ~55). The addition of deep-fried cauliflower, fat, and protein from the Gobi 65 component is expected to moderately lower the overall GI relative to plain rice; GI 55 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (55 × 27.5 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 53.1 g. gi_category set to 'low' per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g; ~1120 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced salt preparation, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1120 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~53) driven by basmati rice carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–175 g) to limit glycemic load, pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the large rice component delivers a substantial carbohydrate load despite the moderate GI of basmati rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil contribution raising total fat and caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should be aware that the deep-fried cauliflower component adds approximately 6.8 g fat per 100 g (predominantly unsaturated), and a full serving contributes roughly 650 kcal; portion control and preference for baked or air-fried variants are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gobi 65 Biryani is an Indian vegetarian rice dish that combines two popular preparations: Gobi 65 (deep-fried spiced cauliflower) and fragrant basmati biryani. Cauliflower florets are marinated in a spiced yogurt and gram-flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then layered or tossed with long-grain basmati rice that has been cooked with whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, bay leaves, and cinnamon. The dish is finished with fried onions, fresh coriander, and mint. The result is a richly aromatic, mildly spicy rice dish with contrasting textures — soft, fluffy rice against crunchy cauliflower. It is popular in South Indian restaurants and Indian diaspora eateries across Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 648 kcal, 14.7 g protein, 23.8 g fat, and 96.3 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notable (~1120 mg per serving), making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low category) based on basmati rice data from the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycemic load per serving is high (~53), which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains dairy (yogurt) and possible gluten from the batter coating.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Gobi 65 Biryani?
What is the GI of Gobi 65 Biryani?
What spices are in Gobi 65 Biryani?
How is Gobi 65 different from regular cauliflower biryani?
Where can I get Gobi 65 Biryani in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Gobi 65 Biryani. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition of ICMR-NIN data for cooked basmati rice and deep-fried cauliflower components, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are primary approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry covers Gobi 65 Biryani as a prepared dish. Values should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: this is a plant-based dish with no animal fat; confirmed zero for cholesterol in a fully plant-based preparation; trans fat is confirmed zero as no hydrogenated oils are used in traditional preparation. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products that supply B12; confirmed zero for a vegetarian dish with no dairy-derived B12 contribution beyond trace. 4. GI set to 55 (low category, GI ≤ 55) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice data. GL per serving = (55 × 27.5 × 350) / 10000 = 53.1. 5. Serving size of 350 g is a typical restaurant plate estimate for this dish in Singapore. 6. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to yogurt in the Gobi 65 marinade. 7. Allergen note: gram flour batter may contain gluten depending on preparation; yogurt is a dairy allergen. 8. Food ID GD002917 from field survey data seed record; served at Mr Tandoor — Boat Quay.
